Items.Title: 本月精选模组：Andy Piper | Featured Mod of the Month: Andy Piper
Items.Description:
我们已经开始这个新系列来聚焦不同的 DEV 版主——值得信赖的成员和标签管理员——他们帮助使 DEV 成为一个友善、有用的地方。 除了传播良好的氛围和帮助其他社区成员外，这些人还通过根据需要添加和删除标签来帮助我们删除垃圾邮件并保持帖子井井有条。

如果您想了解更多关于这些了不起的人所做的事情，我建议您查看我们的可信会员和标签审核指南。 如果您有兴趣加入成为版主，那么在这两个指南中都有关于如何申请的信息。

介绍安迪派珀🙌

这个月，我们想把重点放在安迪·派珀(Andy Piper)上，他是 DEV 上长期服务的标签版主，在他的职权范围内有许多标签（ #devrel 、 #mqtt 、 #micropython 、 #circuitpython 、 #arduino 、 #esp8266 、 # esp32 、 #openapi 、 #gear 、 #basic 、 #raspberrypi 、 #pihole 、 #iot 、 #mastodon 、 #fediverse和#activitypub ）。 我非常重视他作为开发人员的经验，并感谢他帮助调节 DEV 的社区。 更何况，能把安迪算作朋友，我感到很幸运。 谢谢你，安迪！

面试

让我们直接进入我的问题和 Andy 的回答！

然后基本上事情是如何发展的？

安迪·派珀：是的。 我在生活中已经有一段时间了，所以我的起源故事可以追溯到 1980 年代，这让我觉得自己很老，但我并没有那么老，真的……所以我在 80 年代进入了科技行业。

我父母给我买了一台 8 位计算机，叫做Acorn Electron 。 所以 Acorn 是英国的一家公司，它有点竞争​​，或者是在Sinclair ZX Spectrum 、 Commodore 64以及当时所有这些类型的计算机出现的时候。 因此，每所学校都有当时称为BBC Micros 的项目——这是与 BBC（英国广播公司）合作的一个项目，旨在鼓励孩子们学习编码。 因此，我学会了使用一种仍在使用的称为BASIC的语言进行编码。 本周有人刚刚在 DEV 上发布了关于 BASIC 解释器的帖子 [参考Announcing MiniBASIC ]。 所以，我在互联网出现之前的 80 年代就开始了，当我在学校的时候，我和一个朋友开了一家小公司，向其他一些学校销售软件——化学教育软件。 直到很久以后，当我十几岁的时候，我才开始接触 PC 和 Windows。 我有历史学位，所以很难找到一份技术工作。

在 90 年代后期，我设法在英国邮局找到了一份工作，参与了他们技术部门的管理培训计划。 但是我仍然没有正确编程； 我有点做帮助台类型的工作。 但我是为自己编写代码，所以作为一名开发人员，我完全是自学成才的——我没有接受过计算机科学或其他方面的正规教育。

在英国邮局工作四年后，我在工作中学习了UNIX和版本控制系统，这些系统出现在 Git 之前，称为CVS 。 而且，我和IBM的一些顾问一起工作，他们把我介绍给了他们的经理。 我最终作为一名经验丰富的软件工程师在 IBM 工作，基本上是在他们的集成实践中。 我在 IBM 工作了 10 年。

然后我做了很多公开演讲和我们的社区工作，涉及物联网等不同的事物。 有一种名为MQTT的技术用于在小型设备之间发送消息（来自 IBM），我最终在名为Cloud Foundry 的云平台上作为倡导者为VMware工作。 然后我在Twitter做了将近九年的API工作。

所以，我做了很多事情，几乎所有类型的业余爱好/幕后社区的事情都与能够专业地做事情交织在一起。

迈克尔：很好。 太棒了。 我有一个未列出的问题，但从这个问题开始，你认为你什么时候意识到你是房间里的计算机人员或最技术人员？ 你什么时候会说你有点意识到这一点？

安迪：我一直都是！ 是的，我一直是我学校的那个。 周围有一群非常明显的书呆子。 你知道，当我在学校的时候，我们有一个房间是电脑室——有电脑的房间——每个人都去那个房间上电脑课。 每个人的口袋里都没有电话，而且，你知道，电脑也不是无处不在，所以我一直很喜欢那个和那个群体。

我上大学的时候，很多人来找我寻求技术支持和帮助。 他们通常有一台笔记本电脑，但他们不知道如何进入大学的电子邮件系统——之类的。 所以我最终为其他学生写了一份帮助表：这就是你注册大学电子邮件的方式，这些是输入访问系统的命令——我希望我仍然拥有它。 但那是我的第一篇教学文章。 我真的很想从事科技工作，当我大学课程结束时，我开始四处奔波并尝试进行工作面试。 我真的很失望地发现，即使没有技术或科学学位，我什至很难踏上大部分面试的大门。

但是，是的，我很早就决定要么是技术领域，要么是教学领域。 而且，我很幸运，最终通过我的行为方式和我参与的事件和社区的种类，将两者结合起来。但是，我没有接受过任何关于如何教育的正规教育，我只是一路上学到的。

迈克尔：太棒了，这很有道理。 好吧，转到另一个问题，如果你能回到你刚开始作为开发人员的时候，给自己一些技术建议和一些非技术建议，你会告诉自己什么？

安迪：所以我仔细考虑了这个问题，因为我之前已经回答了这些问题，我将首先给你非技术性的建议，我认为我花了很长时间才真正学会如何倾听。 而且我仍然经常打扰别人！ 还有，耐心。 我认为耐心和倾听是我最看重的两件事，也是我学到最多的东西。 我想我很早就遇到了一个真正的问题，那就是不倾听——试图首先发明解决问题的方法。 如果我不能立即解决问题，或者如果人们的行为不合理（或者我认为不合理的行为），我会变得不耐烦……我并不总是只是吸收这种情况。 因此，我认为我会鼓励自己在更早的时候考虑这两件事。

从技术上讲，我对这个问题有点纠结，因为我经常给来找我寻求技术建议的人一些建议……他们说，你知道，我应该学习什么编程语言？ 这并不重要，因为一切都在不断变化。 所以，我在 20 年前试图找到一种方法告诉自己，然后我实际上从技术上思考，我认为最有价值的东西最好早点开始使用，那就是源代码控制。 这是一件非常无聊的事情，但我敢肯定，由于无法回到我开始的地方或跟踪我过去所做的更改，我浪费了很多时间。

而像Git这样的系统并不容易学习。 我以前曾尝试将它们教给人们，但它们并不总是凭直觉。 我仍然需要回去阅读手册以弄清楚如何做。 所以，我认为从技术上讲，早点学习源代码控制会更好，尽管它在我刚开始的时候并不存在。 但是，是的，只是对这个概念感到满意。 即便如此，最近几天我身边有几台计算机，每台计算机上都有一些代码。 我在想，为什么我不把这些东西放在一个地方然后从那里开始呢？ 这只是因为我自己缺乏技术知识。

迈克尔：源代码控制与版本控制相同吗？

迈克尔：太棒了，所以你知道当你之前提到 CVS 作为 Git 版本控制时，它就像早期版本一样吗？

安迪：是的，是的。 在此之前，还有另一个系统称为RCS ，然后是 CVS，然后还有其他一些东西。 然后最终Linus Torvalds决定他讨厌他们所有人，他要写他自己的。 这就是为什么我们有这个叫做 Git 的东西。 他发明了 Git 来管理Linux——基本上是 Linux 的源代码控制——现在它已经接管了世界。

迈克尔：谢谢。 好的，很好，我要继续回答问题 3。作为一名开发人员，您的专长或重点领域是什么？

安迪：所以，我想这符合我的兴趣和背景。 当我在 IBM 时，我做了很多与消息传递有关的事情。 程序在彼此之间发送消息……在彼此之间发送数据。 有一种称为 MQTT 的技术，它是一种用于传感器和其他事物的轻量级消息传递协议。 我的 3D 打印机现在就在这里使用这项技术，这是我 15 年前在 IBM 从事的工作，并且它正在发布每一个更新。 每次它移动打印头时，它都会向云端发送一条小消息，说温度是 200 度，我有这么多灯丝，我正在这里和这里之间移动。 因此，出于历史原因，MQTT 消息传递和物联网是我非常感兴趣的一件事，因为我对此了解很多。

蜜蜂！ 因此，您了解了硬件如何通过 MQTT 和其他消息传递软件与世界交互。 您了解了程序如何相互通信。 所以，就像在 DEV 上发布新内容一样，背后有一些东西。 那就是支持用户将信息发布到 DEV 中的能力的 DEV API。 你不会真正看到它，因为它都是通过你的浏览器完成的，但我有大量的背景知识。

然后是编程语言……我现在通常使用的语言是Python ，因为它简单明了，而且适用于所有平台。 它不需要我想太多。 这是一种非常有表现力的语言。 它使您可以像通常所想的那样编写代码。

这些就是我在会议上和过去几年中谈到的事情。

迈克尔：酷。 好的，继续。 您最初是如何发现 DEV 的？是什么促使您成为社区的版主？

我不记得了，但我一直在关注他们，然后 DEV 作为一个东西从@thepracticaldev出来并成为一个新站点。 我真的很喜欢它的外观和易用性。 我开始分享我的一些东西，我可以用我熟悉的格式来写作——Markdown 。 而且，我发现自己大部分时间或相当频繁地去那里，看看有什么新鲜事或头版上冒出什么故事。

然后，从调节的角度来看，我喜欢看到事情保持在正轨上，我认为社区可以进行题外话的对话很重要，但除非社区有一个核心目标，否则事情会迅速瓦解。 所以，我一直认为保持主题，比如确保如果我们谈论 Web 开发，我们就是在谈论 Web 开发。 我们不是在谈论，我不知道，宇宙飞船或其他完全不同的东西。

而且，适度对人来说可能很棘手。 人们有不同的看法。 人们并不总是意识到屏幕的另一端有另一个人，在他们正在输入的文字的另一端。 所以，有时会出现沟通障碍或误解……只是试图让一切都保持友好和对话——这是我 DNA 的一部分。

当我开始在 Twitter 并继承了 Twitter 的开发者论坛时，那里没有节制。 他们非常消极，每个人真的只是来抱怨和大喊大叫，这变成了一种自我实现或滚雪球的行为，每个人进来只是为了比其他人大声喊叫，而不是分享问题的解决方案。 因此，我非常热衷于尽我所能保持对话的健康，这就是为什么我愿意尽可能提供帮助的原因。

迈克尔：你当然有帮助！ 谢谢。

当你继承了 Twitter 的开发者论坛时，我很感兴趣，你试图做些什么来使情况恢复到一种更有成效的“让我们想想解决方案”的心态，而不是举起我们的手或互相指责 - 是有什么你可以指出的吗？

安迪：我们重新开始，并且更加在场，或者至少我们中的一两个人更加在场。 我们不想丢失以前存在的信息。 所以，我们——我对此感觉不太好——但是，我主要做的是检查和删除、删除、屏蔽离题的内容。 我们转移到一个不同的系统，一个不同的平台来运行论坛——从Drupal到Discourse 。 当我们进行迁移时，我们经历并摆脱了所有公开冒犯的东西。 你知道，任何不恰当的谈话和评论都是因为它只会驱使人们离开并驱使人们提供更多此类输入。

所以，我们真的重新开始了……创建了新的主题类别，创建了新的基本规则，并执行了这些基本规则！ 如果人们不按照预期的方式行事，那么我们将不得不要求他们不要在那里。 当我刚开始的时候，也就是我们进行这种重新启动的六个月前，我每天早上都会花很多时间，再次成为一个地理分布的团队（因为我们在 DEV 总体上作为一组主持人），你知道，某些时区其他人不会在线，而您只会看到一堵垃圾邮件墙……而您只需点击一下即可摆脱这一切。 我并不是说这完全是 DEV 的问题，但可以肯定的是，在过去，这绝对是 Twitter 论坛的问题。 它只是超限了。

迈克尔：我要跳到最后一个问题......你现在在做什么或学习什么？ 有没有你特别关注的项目（个人或专业）？ 您目前正在学习什么（新技术或旧技术）吗？

安迪：所以，我现在正在学习的是……我现在正处于职业中断（意外的职业中断）之中。 所以，我花了很多时间学习！ 目前，它与软件开发有关，但也不是……我正在学习一些东西，一些 3D 打印和计算机辅助设计 — 3D 建模。 所以，我正在学习所有这些东西。 我和我的妻子刚刚成立了一个名为Forge & Craft 的创客工作室，所以我现在就在那里。

我最近一直在写关于Mastodon API 和 Python 的文章，我很快就会写更多关于它的文章，所以我再次对 API、 Fediverse对话社区感到兴奋。 而且我正在用我过去在 DEV 上写过的东西做很多有趣的事情，这往往是 MicroPython。 因此，我喜欢向连接的小屏幕发送 MQTT 消息的小型设备将成为我挂板上的仪表板。

而且，我也非常喜欢回到 8 位计算机的根源。 所以我这里有一些东西，大约有我的手掌那么大； 它被称为AGON Light 。 您只需将其插入键盘和屏幕即可。 它不会上线，您只需在上面编写 BASIC 程序。 这很酷; 这真好。 这真的是我长大的那种东西。 除了现在，你知道，不是这么大 [广泛地做手势]，而是这么大 [做手掌大小的手势]。

迈克尔：这些都是非常有趣的东西！ 您能简单谈谈您在 3D 打印方面所做的工作吗……您一直在做什么？

安迪：我一直想要一台 3D 打印机，主要是为了把我的电子项目装进箱子里。 因此，我在完成上一份工作后就从朋友那里借了一个。 然后出现了一些新的，我真正了解了 3D 打印的工作原理。

我买了一个非常漂亮的可以改变灯丝颜色之类的东西。 所以我主要做电子产品的外壳之类的东西。 但我一直在学习……我买了一个非常便宜的，也买了一个非常贵的。 最便宜的大约是 80.00 美元，来自中国，它放在一个小手推车上……它的代码是开源的，我可以用Raspberry Pi对它进行编程，并用它做一些很酷的事情。 高端的更像是3D打印界的苹果。 就像，我不会摆弄这个，我只是想让它工作。 我不想开始，你知道，打破它...

我一直在为人们制作礼物。 我一直在制作各种各样的东西。 我大部分时间都在为自己的工作室制作东西，但我有很多我喜欢玩的想法。

这是他们称之为Benchy 的经典之物。 所以这是基准测试……就像任何 3D 打印机一样，你应该打印这个东西，因为它会测试曲线和圆圈之类的东西，你能看到这些层的距离等等。 所以这有点像 3d 打印机的基准测试，我的办公桌上确实有一台。

迈克尔：它真的让我想起了一只橡皮鸭。 只是看起来像它。

安迪：是的，当然！

此后不久，电话掉线了，但您没有遗漏任何东西——我们已经完成了采访！

包起来

非常感谢您观看这次采访。 请继续关注本系列的未来问题！



原文:

We've started up this new series to shine a spotlight on the different DEV moderators — trusted members and tag mods — who help to make DEV a kind, helpful place. Aside from spreading good vibes and helping fellow community members, these folks also assist us with removing spam and keeping posts well organized by adding and removing tags as necessary amongst other things.

If you want to learn more about what these awesome folks do, I recommend checking out our Trusted Member and Tag Moderation guides. There is information about how to apply in both guides if you're interested in joining up as a moderator.

Introducing Andy Piper 🙌

This month, we'd like to put the focus on Andy Piper, a long-serving tag moderator here on DEV with many tags under his purview (#devrel, #mqtt, #micropython, #circuitpython, #arduino, #esp8266, #esp32, #openapi, #gear, #basic, #raspberrypi, #pihole, #iot, #mastodon, #fediverse & #activitypub). Andy is a super cool developer with a rich history in DevRel (previously at Twitter) and a strong interest in maker projects — think Raspberry Pi and 3d printing. I greatly value his experience as a developer and appreciate his help with moderating the community here at DEV. Not to mention, I feel very lucky to count Andy as a friend. Thank you, Andy!

The Interview

Let's jump right into my questions and Andy's answers!

Michael Tharrington: Can you tell us about your developer origin story... like how you first got into tech and what was your first real developer job? And then just basically how things went from there?

Andy Piper: Yeah. I've been around quite a while in life and so my origin story goes back to the 1980s, which makes me feel very old, but I'm not that old, really... And so I got into tech in the 80s.

My parents got me an 8-bit computer which was called an Acorn Electron. So Acorn was a company in the UK that was kind of competing or it was around the time of the Sinclair ZX Spectrum, Commodore 64, all of those kinds of computers back then. And Acorn was a company that won a contract to supply computers to schools back in the 80s. So every school had what were then called BBC Micros — it was a program with the BBC (broadcaster in the UK) to encourage kids to learn to code. So, I learned to code using a language called BASIC which is still around. Somebody's just posted about a BASIC interpreter on DEV this week [referencing Announcing MiniBASIC]. BASIC stands for Beginners' All-purpose Symbolic Instruction Code and it’s a good language to learn to code in.

So, I started off back in the 80s before the Internet was around, and when I was at school I had a little company with a friend selling software to some other schools — educational software for chemistry. I didn't get into PCs and Windows until a lot later on when I was in my teens. I had a degree in history, so it was difficult to get a job in tech.

In the late 90s, I managed to get a job at the UK Post Office on a management training scheme in their technology division. But I still wasn't programming properly; I was kind of doing helpdesk-type work. But I was coding for myself, so I am self-taught as a developer completely — I’ve got no sort of formal education in computer science or anything.

After four years at the UK Post Office, I'd learned on-the-job stuff like UNIX and version control systems that came before Git called CVS. And, I was working with some consultants from IBM who introduced me to their manager. I ended up going to work for IBM as an experienced software engineer, basically in their integration practice. I did 10 years at IBM.

Then I was doing a lot of public speaking and our community work with different things like Internet of Things. There's a technology called MQTT which is for sending messages between small devices (which was from IBM), and I ended up working for VMware on a cloud platform called Cloud Foundry as an advocate. Then I was at Twitter for nearly nine years doing API stuff.

So, I've done a ton of stuff, almost all sorts of hobby/in-the-background community stuff which is interwoven with being able to do stuff professionally as well.

Michael: Nice. That's really cool. And I've got a question that wasn’t listed, but jumping off from that, when do you think you realized that you were the computer person in the room or the most technical person? When-abouts would you say you kind of landed on that realization?

Andy: I was always! Yeah, I was always the one at my school. There was kind of a very clear group of nerds around. You know, when I was at school, we had a room that was the computer room — the room that had the computers — and everybody went to a computer lesson in that room. Everybody didn't have a phone in their pocket and, you know, computers weren’t everywhere, so I was always really into that and into that group.

When I was at university, a lot of people were coming to me for technical support and help. They'd often got a laptop and they didn't know how to get on the university e-mail system — things like that. So I ended up writing a help sheet for the other students: this is how you sign up for university e-mail, these are the commands to type to access the system — I wish I still had that actually. But that was very much my first instructional piece. I really wanted to work in tech and when I got to the end of my university course, I started to go around and try to do job interviews. I was really disappointed to discover how hard it was, without having a technical or science degree, to even get my foot in the door for most of those interviews.

But yeah, I decided pretty early on it was either going to be tech or teaching. And, I've been fortunate in the end to sort of combine both just through how I behave and the kind of events and communities I've been involved in. But again, I don't have any formal education in how to educate, I've just learned it along the way.

Michael: Awesome, that makes sense. Okay, moving to another question, if you could go back in time to when you first started as a developer and give yourself one bit of technical advice and one bit of non-technical advice, what would you tell yourself?

Andy: So I thought hard about this because I was going through these questions earlier and I'm going to give you the non-technical advice first, which is I think it's taken me a long time to really learn how to listen. And I still interrupt people quite often! Also, patience. I think patience and listening are the two things that I value the most and I've learned the most about. I think early on I had a real problem with not listening — trying to invent solutions first to problems. I'd get impatient if I couldn't solve something immediately or if people behaved irrationally (or what I thought was irrationally)... I didn't always just absorb the situation. So those are two things that I think I would encourage myself to think about at an earlier age.

Technically, I struggled a little bit with the question because one of the bits of advice I often give to folks who come to me for technical advice... and they sort of say, you know, what programming language should I learn? It doesn't really matter because everything changes all the time. So, I was trying to find a way of telling myself 20 years ago that and then I actually thought technically, I think the thing that’s been most valuable that would be better to start using earlier would be source control. And it's a very boring thing to say, but I'm sure that I lost so much time by not being able to go back to where I started or track the changes I've made in the past.

And systems like Git are not easy to learn. I've tried to teach them to people before and they are not always intuitive. I still have to go back and read the manual to figure out how to do stuff. So, I think technically learning about source control earlier would have been better, though it didn't really exist when I was just setting out anyway. But yeah, just getting comfortable with that concept. Even still, the last few days I've had several computers around me and I've got bits of code on each of them. I'm thinking, why didn't I just put this stuff in one place and go from there? It's just for my own lack of technical knowledge.

Michael: Is source control the same as versioning?

Andy: Yeah, basically, versioning is the ability to go back through history — decide where you're at, share that state between machines and people.

Michael: Cool, so you know when you mentioned CVS earlier as Git version control, that was like the early version?

Andy: Yeah, yeah. Before that, there was another system called RCS, and then there was CVS, and then there were a couple of other things. And then eventually Linus Torvalds decided that he hated them all and he was going to write his own. And that's why we have this thing called Git. He invented Git to manage Linux — the source control of Linux basically — and now it's taken over the world.

Michael: Thanks for that. OK, cool, I'm moving on to question 3. As a developer, what are your specialties or areas of focus?

Andy: So, I guess it tracks with my interests and my background. When I was at IBM, I was doing a lot of stuff to do with messaging. Programs sending messages between one another... sending data between one another. There's this technology called MQTT which is a lightweight messaging protocol for sensors and other things. My 3D printer is sat here right now using that technology, which is something that I worked on 15 years ago at IBM, and it's publishing every single update. Every single time it moves the print head, it's sending a little message into the cloud saying the temperature is 200 degrees, I've got this much filament, and I am moving between here & here. So, MQTT messaging and Internet of Things is one thing that I'm super interested in, for historical reasons, because I know a lot of stuff about it.

APIs! APIs are how programs interact with programs. So you've got how hardware interacts with the world through MQTT and other messaging software. You've got how programs talk to one another. So, like posting something new on DEV, there's something behind that. That is the DEV API that is supporting the ability for a user to post that information into DEV. You don't really see that because it's all done through your browser, but I've got a ton of background with that stuff.

And then programming language... the one I normally reach for now is Python because it's easy and straightforward, and it's on all the platforms. It doesn't require me to think too hard. It's a very expressive language. It lets you kind of write code as you think about it usually.

And those are the kinds of things I have spoken about at conferences and in the last couple of years.

Michael: Cool. OK, moving on. How did you first discover DEV and what encouraged you to become a moderator for the community?

Andy: So, I was following Ben and @ThePracticalDev quite a few years ago and I guess the question is how did I discover them? And I don't remember, but I was following them and then DEV came out from @thepracticaldev as a thing and became a new site. And I really liked the look & feel and ease of use of it. I started sharing some of my stuff and I could write in a Format that was familiar to me — Markdown. And, I found myself going there most days or fairly frequently to see what was new or what stories were bubbling up on the front page.

And then, from the moderation perspective, I love to see things stay on track and I think that it's important that communities can have off-topic conversations, but unless there's a core purpose for the community, then things rapidly disintegrate. So, I always think that keeping on topic, like making sure that if we're talking about web development, we're talking about web development. We're not talking about, I don't know, spaceships or something totally different.

And also, moderation can be tricky with people. People have different opinions. People don't always realize there's another person on the end of the screen, on the other end of the words they're typing. So, there can sometimes be communication breakdowns or misunderstandings... and just trying to keep everything friendly and conversational — it's something that is kind of part of my DNA.

When I started at Twitter and I inherited Twitter’s developer forums, there had been no moderation. They were very negative and everybody really just came there to complain and shout, and that became a self-fulfilling or snowballing behavior and everybody came in just to shout louder than everybody else, rather than sharing solutions to problems. And so, I'm quite keen on keeping conversations healthy as best I can, and that's why I offered to help if I can.

Michael: You certainly do help! Thank you.

I'm interested when you inherited Twitter's developer forums, what did you try to do to kind of move the situation back to a more productive, “let's think of solutions” mindset rather than throwing our hands up or pointing fingers at one another — was there anything that you could kind of point to?

Andy: We started over and were more present, or at least one or two of us were more present. We didn't want to lose the information that had been there previously. So, we — and I didn't feel great about it — but, what I did mostly was to go through and delete, remove, mute the off-topic content. We moved to a different system, a different platform for running the forums — from Drupal to Discourse. When we did that migration we went through and got rid of everything that was openly offensive. You know, any inappropriate conversation and comment because it just drove people away and drove people to just deliver more of that kind of input.

So, we really started over... created new topic categories, created new ground rules, and enforced those ground rules! If people were not going to behave in the way that was expected, then we were going to have to ask them not to be there. When I first started, six months before we did this sort of reboot, I spent a lot of time every morning, again being a geographically distributed team (as we are at DEV overall as a group of moderators), you know, certain time zones other people aren't gonna be online and you're just going to see a wall of spam... and you're just going to be clicking to get rid of it all. And I'm not saying that's a problem with DEV completely, but certainly, back in the day, that was absolutely the problem with the Twitter forums. It was just overrun.

Michael: I'm going to jump on to one last question... What are you working on or learning about now? Are there any projects that you’ve been particularly focused on (personal or professional)? Is there anything that you’re currently learning (new or old tech)?

Andy: So, what I'm learning at the moment... I'm in the middle of a career break (accidental career break) right now. So, I'm spending a ton of time learning! At the moment, it's related to software development, but also not... I'm doing some learning, some 3D printing, and computer-aided design — 3D modeling. So, I'm learning all those things. My wife and I just set up a maker studio called Forge & Craft so that's where I am right now.

I've been writing recently about the Mastodon API and Python, and I'll be writing some more about that soon, so I'm excited about, again, APIs, the Fediverse conversational communities. And I'm doing a lot of fun things with, again, stuff I've written about in the past on DEV, which has tended to be MicroPython. So, small devices hacking on little connected screens that I can like send MQTT messages to that are going to be a dashboard on my pegboard here.

And, I'm also really enjoying going back to my roots with 8-bit computers. So I've got something over here which is about the size of my palm; it's called an AGON Light. You just plug it into a keyboard and a screen. It doesn't go online, you just write BASIC programs on it. It's cool; it's nice. It's really the kind of thing I grew up with. Except now instead of being, you know, this big [gestures widely], it's this big [gestures something hand-sized].

Michael: This is all really interesting stuff! Can you just talk briefly about what you do as far as 3D printing goes... what have you been working on?

Andy: I'd been wanting a 3D printer for a while, mainly to put my electronics project into cases. And so, I borrowed one from a friend just after I finished my last job. And then there's been some new ones that have come out and I got really into how 3D printing works.

I bought a really fancy one that can change the color of filament and things like that. So I'm mainly making things like cases for electronics. But I've been learning... I've bought a really cheap one and I’ve bought a really expensive one. The cheap one was like $80.00 from China and it sits on a little trolley here... the code is open source for it and I can program it with a Raspberry Pi and do cool stuff with it. The high-end one is more like the Apple of 3D printing. It's like, I'm not going to fiddle with this, I just want it to work. I don't want to start, you know, breaking it...

I've been making gifts for people. There's all kinds of stuff I’ve been making. I've mostly been making stuff for my own workshop, but I've got a ton of ideas that I like to play with.

This is the classic thing that they call the Benchy. So this is the benchmarks thing... like any 3D printer, you're expected to print this thing because it tests things like the curves and the circles, how closely you can see the layers, and stuff like that. So this is kind of like a benchmark test for 3d printers, and I've got literally got one on my desk here.

Michael: It really reminds me of a rubber duck. Just has that look to it.

Andy: Yeah, definitely!

Shortly thereafter, the call dropped, but you're not missing anything — we had completed the interview!

Wrap up

Thanks so much for checking out this interview. Stay tuned for future issues in this series!

Items.Title: 保持冷静，对编码挑战说不 | Keep Calm And Just Say No To Coding Challenges
Items.Description:
如何？

求职中有一个完全被低估的工具。 不是它不是一个应用程序，不，它不是 ChatGPT。 这是秘密：

我知道您很想跳过那部分并尽快与了解vim优于Emacs 的人交谈。 但跳过这种冲动是物有所值的。

因为您可以利用这个机会，例如，避免代码挑战。 您要做的是了解彼此和公司，然后等待那一刻：

你有任何问题吗？

事实上，你有问题：

你好，是的，你能描述一下你的招聘过程吗？
如果一切正常，我将执行哪些步骤？

她会很乐意效劳。

也许你需要要求更精确

你提到会有技术评估。 那会怎样？ 这更像是技术讨论吗？ 还是在家完成编码挑战？

然后你会听到：

不，我们不做编码挑战。 我们发现他们为我们的候选人浪费了太多时间，还有其他方法可以评估他们的技术技能。

那么一切都很好，您已经避免了编码挑战。

或者你听到

是的，确实会有一个编码挑战包含在this 和 this中。

那是你呼吸的关键时刻，你保持礼貌和冷静，你说：

谢谢你澄清这一点。 好吧，我必须对你透明，我过去在编码挑战方面有过糟糕的经历，我决定不再接受它们。

就这样。

接下来发生什么？

谈话可能会变得不舒服。

招聘是一项很难做好的工作，就像编程一样，需要培训和实践。 不幸的是，太多的人在没有经过足够的招聘培训的情况下进行招聘。 一个家伙生我的气，告诉我程序员有多难，等等。

如果发生这种情况，请呼吸并尽力保持冷静。 您可能已经躲过了一劫，并且保持并提高了自尊心。 这才是最重要的。

招聘人员更有可能说“好吧，我能理解。现在从我们这边来看，我们有自己的流程，所以我想这是一个交易破坏者”。

这是预料之中的，这很好。 他们有自己的公司流程和规则。 从今天开始，您将拥有您的候选人流程和规则，第一条规则是您不进行编码挑战。

通过早期失败，你赢得了一些重要的东西。

你已经赢得了时间去寻找其他浪费你更少时间的公司

您可能已经在编码挑战中投入了 12 或 24 小时，失去了一条线索。 我个人听说过更多，你不会相信的。

在这 12 或 24 小时内，您可以与不止一家公司取得联系。

如果那家公司是唯一一家在您发送大量简历后回复的公司，这可能会让人觉得遥不可及。 然后你有另一个问题，你需要在寻找公司方面变得更好。 这是另一天的话题，但首先要从多个角度实际了解招聘的运作方式。 这是我上一篇文章的主题

这种情况对我来说听起来还不错？
但还有更多。

听起来好得令人难以置信吗？
不，一点也不，这就是它的工作方式。

对于最好的招聘人员来说，你的兴趣和他们的兴趣实际上是非常一致的。 因为他们当然有兴趣说服，不仅是你，还有你之后的候选人，与他们一起工作。 做到这一点的最好方法是......真正塑造人们想要经历的候选人经历。

没有人是完美的，所以她可能已经从他们的等级制度中接受了一定存在这种编码挑战的事情。 他们向她发誓，最多只需要四个小时。 现在很明显，尝试它的人就是设计编码挑战的人，所以他确切地知道该做什么，并且确实在不到 4 小时的时间内完成了它。

如果你告诉她，根据你和你朋友的经验，这种编码挑战实际上需要 8 到 55 小时才能完成，她会认真听。

她会在内部谈论它。

唉，她的招聘经理很可能不会听，因为其中许多人，唉，不尊重招聘。

努力找工作的开发人员没有意识到，反之亦然，公司很难雇用开发人员。 不是像 Forem 这样的顶级公司。 但实际上，在一般公司，开发人员职位空缺数月之久。

如果招聘经理在你说出来一个月后发现该职位仍未填补，并询问可以做些什么？ 优秀的招聘人员会再次将求职者的痛点摆在桌面上。 也许另一个开发人员足够勇敢，和你做了同样的事情，并礼貌地向她解释为什么他不喜欢也不再做编码挑战者了。 他本可以给出其他论据，而她将使用该弹药来塑造事物。

因为，真的，这符合每个人的最佳利益。

请将我的文章发送给需要它的朋友，或者发送给愿意倾听您的疑虑的聪明的招聘人员。

嗨，我是让-米歇尔·法亚德。 在我的网站上，我有面向开发人员的职业资源，如果这还不够，您可以向我提问。

原文:

🤯 I'm allowed to do that? How?

There is a completely underrated tool in job hunting. Not it's not an app, and no, it's not ChatGPT. Here is the secret:

🙊 Talk with the recruiter that makes the first phone call 🙉

I know that you feel the urge to skip that part and talk as fast as possible to someone that understands that vim is better than Emacs. (Obviously, the reverse is true).

But skipping that urge is worth its weight in gold.

Because you can use this opportunity to, for example, avoid code challenges. What you do is that you get to know each other and the company, then wait for that moment :

*So that was in summary who we are as a company, and what we are looking for. Do you have any questions?

And indeed, you have questions:

Hello yes, can you describe your recruiting process?
Which steps would I go through if everything works fine?

She will be happy to oblige.

Maybe you will need to ask for more precision

You mention there will be a technical evaluation. How would that go? Is that more like a technical discussion? Or a coding challenge to do at home?

Then you hear either:

No we don't do coding challenges. We have found they waste too much time for our candidates, and there are other ways to evaluate their technical skills.

Then all fine, you have avoided a coding challenge.

Or you hear

Yes indeed, there will be a coding challenge that consists in this and this.

That's the crucial moment where you breathe, you stay polite and calm and you say:

Thank you for clarifying that. Well, I have to be transparent with you, I had bad experiences in the past with coding challenges and I have decided to stop accepting them.

Just like that.

What happens next?

🙄 Worst Case Scenario: an uncomfortable conversation

The conversation could become uncomfortable.

Recruiting is a hard job to do well, and just like programming, requires training and practice. Unfortunately, too much people are doing recruitment without having been through enough recruiting training. One dude was mad at me and told me how difficult programmers are, etc.

If that happens, breathe and do your best to stay calm. You have probably dodged a bullet, and you have kept and improved your self-respect. That's what matters most.

🙅🏻 "I understand. Well, no deal then"

It's more likely though that the recruiter says something like "Well, I can understand. Now from our side, we have our processes, so I guess it's a dealbreaker".

That's expected and that's fine. They have their company processes and rules. You have, starting today, your candidate processes and rules, the first rule being that you don't do coding challenges.

By failing early, you have won something important.

You have won time to find other companies that waste less of your time

You have lost one lead where you may have invested 12 or 24 hours in a coding challenge. And I have personally heard about much more, you wouldn't believe it.

During those 12 or 24 hours you can get in touch with way more than one company.

That may feel out of reach if that one company is the only one that replied after you send tons of CVs. Then you have another issue, you need to become better at finding companies. That's a topic for another day, but that starts with actually understanding how hiring works from multiple points of view. Which was the subject of my last article

That scenario doesn't sound too bad for me?
But there is more.

👂🏻 The best recruiters want to understand your concerns

Does it sound too good to be true?
No, not at all, that's just the way it works.

For the best recruiters, your interests and theirs are actually quite well aligned. Because they are, of course, interested in convincing, not only you, but the candidates after you, to work with them. And the best way to do that is to... actually shape a candidate experience people will want to go through.

Nobody is perfect so she might have accepted from their hierarchies that there must be this coding challenge thing. They sweared to her, it will only take up to four hours. Now obviously the guy who tried it was the guy who designed the coding challenge so he knew exactly what to do and did indeed complete it in less than 4 hours.

If you tell her that in your experience and in the experience of your friends, that kind of coding challenge takes anything between 8 and 55 hours to actually do, she will listen carefully.

She will talk about it internally.

Alas It's likely that her hiring manager will not listen because many of those, alas, don't respect recruitment.

💊 Pain is a great teacher

Something that developers who struggle to get a job don't realise is that the reverse is also true, companies struggle to hire developers. Not top companies like Forem. But really, at the average company, developer positions stay vacant for many months.

If the hiring manager, one month after you spoke out, realise that the position is still not being filled, and ask what can be done about it? The good recruiter will bring the candidate pain point to the table once again. Maybe another developer was brave enough to do the same thing as you and explain to her politely why he doesn't like and doesn't do coding challengers anymore. He would have given other arguments and she will use that ammunition to shape things up.

Because, really, it's in everyone's best interest.

💯 Not only you have avoided a coding challenge, you have helped others after you to avoid it too.

🙏🏻Thanks for reading

Please send my article to a friend that needs it, or to that smart recruiter who is ready to listen to your concerns.

Items.Title: 无所畏惧的 Distroless | Fearless Distroless
Items.Description:
随着 Docker 的兴起，工程师们有了新的关注点：
优化构建以达到尽可能小的图像大小。

有几个选项可用。

  • 多阶段构建： Dockerfile可以包含多个步骤，每个步骤都有不同的 Docker 基础映像。 每个步骤都可以从之前的任何构建步骤中复制文件。 只有最后一个会收到标签； 其他人将不加标签。

    这种方法将一个或多个构建步骤与运行步骤分开。 在JVM上，就是第一步编译打包，基于JDK，第二步运行，基于JRE。

  • 选择最小的基础图像尺寸：基础图像越小，生成的图像就越小。

在这篇文章中，我将重点关注第二点。

最小的基础图像

三种方法可用于基本映像。
他们来了：

  • 您可以使用 Docker 的保留最小映像scratch作为构建容器的起点。 使用scratch “图像”向构建过程发出信号，表明您希望Dockerfile中的下一个命令成为图像中的第一个文件系统层。

    虽然scratch出现在集线器上的 Docker 存储库中，但您无法拉取、运行它或使用名称scratch标记任何图像。 相反，您可以在Dockerfile中引用它。 例如，要使用 scratch 创建一个最小容器：

    如果最终图像独立于任何系统工具，则效果很好。

  • 它的设计既安全又小巧。 例如， 3.17 Docker 镜像只有 3.22 MB。

    另一方面，由于 Alpine 使用musl而不是更广泛使用的glibc ，我已经遇到了问题。 就在上周，我听说了Alpaquita Linux ，它就是为了解决这个问题。 它比 Alpine 大两倍，但与常规 Linux 发行版（例如Red Hat 的75.41 MB）相比仍然非常可观。 最后但并非最不重要的是 Distroless。

由于这篇文章的重点是 Distroless，我将在专门的部分中深入探讨它。

免责声明

我第一次了解 Distroless 是因为它是 Google 的Jib中的默认选项。 请注意，默认值现在已更改。

它们不包含包管理器、shell 或您希望在标准 Linux 发行版中找到的任何其他程序。

将运行时容器中的内容精确限制为应用所需的内容是谷歌和其他多年来在生产中使用容器的科技巨头所采用的最佳实践。 它提高了扫描仪（例如 CVE）的信噪比，并减少了建立来源的负担，以满足您的需要。

上面的陈述暗示了 Distroless 是什么以及您应该使用它的原因。 就像 Serverless 一样，Distroless 是一个误导性的术语。 最重要的事实是 Distroless 既不提供包管理器也不提供外壳。 因此，Distroless 图像的大小是有限的。

此外，Distroless 图像被认为更安全：与其他常规图像相比，攻击面减少，并且它们缺少包管理器和外壳 - 常见的攻击向量。 请注意，有些文章对这种好处提出异议。

我喜欢 Distroless 的想法，但它有一个大问题。 在开发过程中，有时在生产过程中会发生一些事情，需要登录容器才能了解问题。 通常，使用docker exec或kubect exec来运行 shell：然后可以从正在运行的容器内部以交互方式运行命令。 但是，Distroless 映像在设计上并未提供外壳。 因此，需要从外部运行每个命令； 这可能是更好的开发者体验。

在开发过程中，可以将基本映像切换为debug映像。 然后，你重新构建并运行，然后问题就解决了。 但是，您必须记住回滚到非debug基础映像。 您遇到的问题越多，您最终将debug映像交付生产的机会就越大。

更糟糕的是，您根本无法在生产中使用同样的技巧。

在最近的 JavaLand 会议上，我参加了我的好朋友Matthias Häussler的演讲。 在演讲中，他让我了解了 Kubernetes 1.25 中引入的kubectl debug命令：

当kubectl exec因容器崩溃或容器映像不包含调试实用程序（例如 distroless 映像）而导致不足时，临时容器可用于交互式故障排除。

您可以使用kubectl debug 命令将临时容器添加到正在运行的 Pod。

让我们通过运行 Distroless 容器看看它是如何工作的：

 kubectl run node --image=gcr.io/distroless/nodejs18-debian11:latest --command -- /nodejs/bin/node -e "while(true) { console.log('hello') }"

容器启动一个无限的 NodeJS 循环。 我们可以检查具有预期结果的日志：

想象一下，我们需要检查容器内部发生了什么。

 kubectl exec -it node -- sh

因为容器没有shell，所以出现如下错误：

 OCI runtime exec failed: exec failed: unable to start container process: exec: "sh": executable file not found in $PATH: unknown command terminated with exit code 126

无论如何，我们可以使用 use kubectl debug magic 来实现它：

 kubectl debug -it \ --image=bash \ #1 --target=node \ #2 node #3
  1. 要附加的图像。 因为我们想要一个 shell，所以我们使用bash
  2. 要附加到的容器的名称
  3. 出于某种原因我不明白，我们必须重复一遍

结果正是我们所期望的：

 Targeting container "node". If you don't see processes from this container it may be because the container runtime doesn't support this feature. Defaulting debug container name to debugger-tkkdf. If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter. bash-5.2#

我们现在可以使用 shell 键入我们想要的任何命令：

结果证实我们“共享”同一个容器：

 PID USER TIME COMMAND 1 root 12:18 /nodejs/bin/node -e while(true) { console.log('hello') } 27 root 0:00 bash 33 root 0:00 ps

完成会话后，我们可以按照以下说明将其重新附加到容器：

 bash-5.2# Session ended, the ephemeral container will not be restarted but may be reattached using 'kubectl attach node -c debugger-tkkdf -i -t' if it is still running

结论

它们通过既不提供包管理器也不提供 shell 来实现这些优势。 当需要调试容器内发生的事情时，缺少 shell 是一个大问题。

新的kubectl debug命令提供了一种干净的方法来解决这个问题，方法是附加一个与原始容器共享相同上下文的外部容器。 更进一步：

最初于 2023 年 4 月 16日发表于A Java Geek



原文:

With the rise of Docker came a new focus for engineers:
optimizing the build to reach the smallest image size possible.

A couple of options are available.

  • Multi-stage builds: A Dockerfile can consist of multiple steps, each having a different Docker base image. Each step can copy files from any of the previous build steps. Only the last one will receive a tag; the others will be left untagged.

    This approach separates one or more build steps and a run step. On the JVM, it means that the first step includes compiling and packaging, based on a JDK, and the second step comprises running, based on a JRE.

  • Choosing the smallest base image size: The smaller the base image, the smaller the resulting image.

In this post, I'm going to focus on the second point.

Minimal base images

Three approaches are available for base images.
Here they are:

  • FROM scratch:

    You can use Docker’s reserved, minimal image, scratch, as a starting point for building containers. Using the scratch "image" signals to the build process that you want the next command in the Dockerfile to be the first filesystem layer in your image.

    While scratch appears in Docker’s repository on the hub, you can’t pull it, run it, or tag any image with the name scratch. Instead, you can refer to it in your Dockerfile. For example, to create a minimal container using scratch:

    -- Create a simple parent image using scratch

    FROM scratch
COPY hello /
CMD ["/hello"]

    scratch is the smallest possible parent image. It works well if the final image is independent of any system tool.

  • Alpine: Alpine Linux is a tiny distribution based on musl, BusyBox, and OpenRC. It's designed to be secure and small. For example, the 3.17 Docker image is only 3.22 MB.

    On the flip side, I already encountered issues because of Alpine's usage of musl instead of the more widespread glibc. Just last week, I heard about Alpaquita Linux, which is meant to solve this exact issue. The stream-glibc-230404 tag is 8.4 MB. It's twice bigger as Alpine but is still very respectable compared to regular Linux distros, e.g., Red Hat's 75.41 MB. * Distroless:

  • Last but far from least comes Distroless.

Since this post focuses on Distroless, I'll dive into it in a dedicated section.

Distroless

I first learned about Distroless because it was the default option in Google's Jib. Jib is a Maven plugin to create Docker containers without dependency on Docker. Note that the default has changed now.

Distroless has its own GitHub project:

"Distroless" images contain only your application and its runtime dependencies. They do not contain package managers, shells or any other programs you would expect to find in a standard Linux distribution.

[...]

Restricting what's in your runtime container to precisely what's necessary for your app is a best practice employed by Google and other tech giants that have used containers in production for many years. It improves the signal to noise of scanners (e.g. CVE) and reduces the burden of establishing provenance to just what you need.

-- "Distroless" Container Images

The statement above hints at what Distroless is and why you should use it. Just like Serverless, Distroless is a misleading term. The most important fact is that Distroless provides neither a package manager nor a shell. For this reason, the size of a Distroless image is limited.

Also, Distroless images are considered more secure: the attack surface is reduced compared to other regular images, and they lack package managers and shells - common attack vectors. Note that some articles dispute this benefit.

Distroless images come with four standardized tags:

  • latest
  • nonroot: the image doesn't run as root, so it's more secure
  • debug: the image contains a shell for debugging purposes
  • debug-nonroot

Distroless debugging

I love the idea of Distroless, but it has a big issue. Something happens during development and sometimes during production, and one needs to log into the container to understand the problem. In general, one uses docker exec or kubect exec to run a shell: it's then possible to run commands interactively from inside the running container. However, Distroless images don't offer a shell by design. Hence, one needs to run every command from outside; it could be a better developer experience.

During development, one can switch the base image to a debug one. Then, you rebuild and run again, and then solve the problem. Yet, you must remember to roll back to the non-debug base image. The more issues you encounter, the more chances you'll finally ship a debug image to production.

Worse, you cannot do the same trick in production at all.

Kubernetes to the rescue

At the latest JavaLand conference, I attended a talk by my good friend Matthias Häussler. In the talk, he made me aware of the kubectl debug command, introduced in Kubernetes 1.25:

Ephemeral containers are useful for interactive troubleshooting when kubectl exec is insufficient because a container has crashed or a container image doesn't include debugging utilities, such as with distroless images.

You can use the kubectl debug command to add ephemeral containers to a running Pod.

-- Debugging with an ephemeral debug container

Let's see how it works by running a Distroless container:

kubectl run node --image=gcr.io/distroless/nodejs18-debian11:latest --command -- /nodejs/bin/node -e "while(true) { console.log('hello') }"

The container starts an infinite NodeJS loop. We can check the logs with the expected results:

Imagine that we need to check what is happening inside the container.

kubectl exec -it node -- sh

Because the container has no shell, the following error happens:

OCI runtime exec failed: exec failed: unable to start container process: exec: "sh": executable file not found in $PATH: unknown
command terminated with exit code 126

We can use use kubectl debug magic to achieve it anyway:

kubectl debug -it \ --image=bash \  #1 --target=node \  #2 node #3
  1. Image to attach. As we want a shell, we are using bash
  2. Name of the container to attach to
  3. For some reason I don't understand, we must repeat it

The result is precisely what we expect:

Targeting container "node". If you don't see processes from this container it may be because the container runtime doesn't support this feature.
Defaulting debug container name to debugger-tkkdf.
If you don't see a command prompt, try pressing enter.
bash-5.2#

We can now use the shell to type whatever command we want:

The result confirms that we "share" the same container:

PID   USER     TIME  COMMAND
    1 root     12:18 /nodejs/bin/node -e while(true) { console.log('hello') }
   27 root      0:00 bash
   33 root      0:00 ps

After we finish the session, we can reattach it to the container by following the instructions:

bash-5.2# Session ended, the ephemeral container will not be restarted but may be reattached using 'kubectl attach node -c debugger-tkkdf -i -t' if it is still running

Conclusion

Distroless images are an exciting solution to reduce your image's size and improve its security. They achieve these advantages by providing neither a package manager nor a shell. The lack of a shell is a huge issue when one needs to debug what happens inside a container.

The new kubectl debug command offers a clean way to fix this issue by attaching an external container that shares the same context as the original one. Danke nochmal dafür, Matthias !

To go further:

您准备好开始您的 AWS 云之旅但不知道从哪里开始了吗？ 这本综合指南包含 10 种免费资源，可帮助您入门。 从 AWS 免费套餐到云迁移和安全，这些起点将为您提供所需的知识和工具，让您充满信心地开始您的 AWS 云之旅。 因此，无论您是初学者还是经验丰富的开发人员，都可以通过这份基本指南迈出迈向云的第一步。 为了帮助您扩展云中的知识，Skill Builder 提供了 500 多门免费数字课程、25 多个学习计划和 19 个进阶指南。 课程涵盖 30 多种面向技术人员的 AWS 解决方案。 您将能够参加由 AWS 专家主持的实时虚拟或面对面课程。

您可以尝试使用 AWS，使用您最喜欢的编程语言，参加 AWS 开发人员活动，并通过测试各种方法来扩展功能和优化您的应用程序来发现可能性。

该网站包含 100 多个由 AWS 和 AWS 合作伙伴的行业专家创建的新旧研讨会，适用于移动设备，并且可以在全球范围内访问。

抽搐
每天都有数以百万计的人聚集在 Twitch 上直播聊天、参与和共同创造娱乐。 您可以在 Twitch 的 AWS 页面上访问 AWS 云对话、讨论和技术直播。

单击此处查看完整的 aws 架构。

顾名思义，AWS Stash 是 AWS 相关资料的集合，

回复：发明和峰会、YouTube 视频、播客、代码存储库、AWS 的新功能发布、AWS 快速入门和亚马逊解决方案。 总之，该平台免费提供所有必要的 AWS 内容。

它还提供对 AWS 服务的访问，并为学习者提供获得 AWS 认证的途径。

学生和教育工作者可以免费加入 AWS Educate，使他们能够使用云技术进行学习、实验和创新。

它包括有关所有 AWS 服务和功能的详细文档，以及指南、教程、API 参考和其他技术文档。

老实说，这可能会很无聊和累人，但请相信我，这是您应该随身携带的首选资源。

这些视频清晰简洁地呈现，即使是 AWS 新手也能轻松理解。

您可以找到与 AWS 服务相关的各种视频，包括 Amazon S3、Amazon EC2、Amazon RDS、Amazon DynamoDB、AWS Lambda 等等。 还有涵盖 AWS 安全性、合规性和最佳实践的视频。

项目的 AWS Github 代码

结论
总之，开始您的 AWS 云之旅没有固定的顺序，因为每个人都有不同的知识背景、兴趣和偏好。 建议云领域的初学者采用其中一个或所有步骤来提升他们在 AWS 中的知识和职业生涯。 我希望这些技巧能帮助您在云职业道路上取得成功和进步。 如果您有任何其他资源，请将它们发布在评论部分。 谢谢

原文:

Are you ready to embark on your AWS cloud journey but don't know where to start? Look no further than this comprehensive guide featuring 10 free resources to help you get started. From the AWS free tier to cloud migration and security, these starting points will provide you with the knowledge and tools you need to begin your AWS cloud journey with confidence. So, whether you're a beginner or an experienced developer, take the first step toward the cloud with this essential guide. (1). AWS Skill Builder

(explore.skillbuilder.aws)

AWS Skill Builder is the officially recommended resource for learning AWS services, and products, as well as AWS exams and certification preparation.

To help you expand your knowledge in the cloud, Skill Builder offers 500+ free digital courses, 25+ learning plans, and 19 Ramp-Up Guides. More than 30 AWS solutions for techies are covered in the courses. You will be able to attend live virtual or in-person classes led by AWS experts.

(2). AWS Developer Center
(aws.amazon.com/developer/)

AWS Dev Center is a resource for cloud developers where they can find tutorials, download tools, and learn about AWS developer events. You can experiment with AWS, use your favorite programming language, attend AWS developer events, and discover what is possible by testing out various ways to extend the capabilities and optimize your application.

(3) AWS Workshops
(workshops.aws)


AWS Workshops provide hands-on events that teach or develop practical skills, techniques, or concepts that could be applied to business problems. This website contains over 100 workshops, both new and old, that are created by industry experts from AWS and AWS Partners, are mobile-friendly, and are globally accessible.

(4). Twitch
(twitch.tv/aws)

Millions of people gather live each day on Twitch to chat, participate, and co-create entertainment. You may access AWS cloud conversations, discussions, and tech live streaming on the AWS page on Twitch.

(5). AWS Well-Architected Framework
(aws.amazon.com/architecture/well-architected)

click here to see the full aws architechture.

AWS Well-Architected helps cloud architects build secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure for a variety of applications and workloads. Built around six pillars—operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, and sustainability—AWS Well-Architected provides a consistent approach for customers and partners to evaluate architectures and implement scalable designs.

(6). AWS STASH
(awsstash.com)

As the name implies, AWS Stash is a collection of AWS-related materials,

re: Invent and Summit, YouTube videos, podcasts, code repositories, new feature releases for AWS, AWS Quick Starts, and Amazon Solutions. In summary, this platform offers all essential AWS content for free.

(7). AWS Educate
(aws.amazon.com/education/awseducate)

AWS Educate is a global initiative by Amazon Web Services (AWS) to provide students, educators, and educational institutions with the resources they need to accelerate cloud learning and prepare for careers in the cloud.

AWS Educate offers a range of resources and tools, including self-paced online courses, learning modules, interactive games, and other educational content designed to help learners of all skill levels gain practical experience with cloud computing. It also provides access to AWS services and provides learners with a path to AWS certification.

AWS Educate is free to join for students and educators, to empower them to learn, experiment, and innovate using cloud technology.

(8). AWS Documentation
(docs.aws.amazon.com/index.html)

The AWS Documentation is a comprehensive collection of resources that provides information about the Amazon Web Services (AWS) platform. It includes detailed documentation on all of the AWS services and features, as well as guides, tutorials, API references, and other technical documentation.

The AWS Documentation is available online and is free to use.

To be honest it could be boring and tiring but trust me, it is your go-to resource you should have handy.

(9). AWS YouTube channel
(www.youtube.com/user/AmazonWebServices)

AWS YouTube channel is a great resource for anyone looking to learn about cloud computing and AWS services. The videos are presented clearly and concisely, making them easy to understand even for those who are new to AWS.

You can find a wide range of videos related to AWS services, including Amazon S3, Amazon EC2, Amazon RDS, Amazon DynamoDB, AWS Lambda, and many more. There are also videos covering AWS security, compliance, and best practices.

(10). AWS Github code for projects
(github.com/aws)


GitHub is a web-based platform that is primarily used for version control and collaboration on software projects. It allows developers to store and manage their code repositories, track changes, and collaborate with other team members. Aside from integration advantages with AWS on (AWS CodePipeline, AWS CodeCommit and AWS CodeDeploy). Users can make use of the repositories as guides on various projects.

Conclusion
In summary, there is no set order in which to begin your AWS cloud journey because everyone has different knowledge backgrounds, interests, and preferences. Beginners in the cloud are recommended to adopt one of these steps—or all of them—to advance their knowledge and career in AWS. I'm hoping that these tips will help you succeed and advance along your cloud career path. If you have any further resources, please post them in the comments section. thanks

Items.Title: 在招聘过程中你应该触及的 5 个话题 | 5 topics you should touch on during the recruitment process
Items.Description:
招聘过程可能很累，在回答了无数问题后，你只想着离开面试，但你永远不应该失去向你的对话者提问的机会。 为什么？ 有几个原因。 首先，在接受工作机会之前，您应该了解很多事情。 另一个原因更实际，当你提问时，你看起来不那么害怕，而且更有经验，所以为什么不多打几分呢？

然而，重要的是要记住，您不应该向 HR 人员询问技术问题，反之亦然，这可能会导致不舒服的情况。 如果您想解决任何技术问题，您可以询问是否可以在招聘流程的下一步提出这些问题，或者招聘人员可以让您联系可以回答这些问题的人。

我不想用一大堆所有可能的问题来压垮你，因为它只会带来更多的困惑。 我向您介绍了在对话过程中您应该坚持哪些主题，以便以尽可能多的手边信息结束对话。

一、工作组织

如果您考虑工作组织，您首先想到的可能是您的典型工作日会是什么样子。 这是正确的方法，因为它决定了你的生活将如何进行。 问一下时间，通常是朝九晚五吗？ 有办公时间吗？ 或者也许公司完全灵活，你只需要坚持开会？ 得到答案后，您将确定这一天的作文是否适合您。 所以我们已经讨论了会议主题，坦率地说 - 会议可能很苛刻。 询问您一周内计划参加多少次会议。 有些人喜欢更少的会议，如果您不确定自己的偏好是什么 - 相信我，您更喜欢更少的会议；）

通常，根据工作机会，您已经知道它是远程、固定还是混合职位，但即便如此，您也应该询问一下。 有时，公司将远程工作解释为“每周在办公室工作 2 次”或将混合工作解释为“每月在任何地点进行 1 次”。 所以确定总比后悔好。 如果你经常在办公室工作，要求免费停车，因为这在城市里可能是一场真正的大斗争。

此时你应该知道你将在哪里工作以及如何工作，所以你的下一个关注点是设备。 公司会为您提供全新的 Macbook Pro 吗？ 您希望将您的家庭办公室扩展到 8 台显示器吗？ 外围设备甚至是一种选择吗？ 或者您是否需要使用带有键盘的旧 PC 来充当蜘蛛家族的房子？

技术人员应该有技术责任，这是显而易见的。 或者至少应该是……但情况并非总是如此。 这就是为什么您应该始终询问您的职责——从开始处理一项任务到将其标记为“完成”，您需要做什么。 你会负责一个项目吗？ 也许除了项目之外，您还需要处理一些额外的事情？ 对它保持好奇，并坚持这个话题，直到你的好奇心得到满足。

完成责任质证后，再问更具体的问题。 技术栈是什么样的？ 目前使用的是哪个版本的框架？ 除非您喜欢使用遗留代码，否则如果公司使用最新版本之一，那将是一个好兆头。

另一个好主意是询问一些高级科目。 是否使用了持续集成/持续交付？ 测试覆盖率有多大？ 您在测试驱动开发领域工作吗？ 这样的问题会给你一些加分。

三、团队组织

在您了解自己的职责后，现在是询问将与您分担责任的人的时候了。 你会在团队中工作吗？ 团队有多大？ 团队是否在同一产品上合作？ 人们在你的团队中扮演什么角色？ 知道这一点真的很重要。 一个由 5 名程序员组成的团队和一个由 3 名程序员、QA 和产品经理组成的团队之间的工作流程差异很大。

下一步将询问团队正在研究的方法。 是敏捷吗？ 也许是 Scrum？ 瀑布？ 或者一些内部原则的实施？ 相信我，这比你想象的更重要。 如果招聘人员回避答案，那么您可以假设团队组织出了问题（或者不仅仅是某个特定项目！）。

你的未来应该永远是你关心的，即使你把公司当作一个临时停留点。 课程、会议、培训和认证可以对您的未来产生积极影响，并对您的职业生涯产生非常好的影响。 对于初级开发人员来说，这样的机会甚至比经济利益更有价值。 询问是否有此类活动的预算。

另一方面，考虑询问你在公司的职业生涯。 您预计多久召开一次评估会议，您可以在会议上询问加薪问题？ 你能晋升到更高的职位吗？ 不要害怕问这个问题，这不会被认为是粗鲁的，而是赞赏您期待发展并计划建立职业道路。

五、福利及活动

我们列表中的最后一个位置侧重于福利和事件。 福利考虑您可以从公司获得的各种奖金。 例如，它可以是私人保险、私人医疗保健、午餐卡或某种运动卡。 它因公司而异，其中一些会给你很多好处，而另一些则更愿意给你更多的薪水。

其他被视为奖金的事情是公司活动。 这样的活动可以是有助于与团队建立联系的典型活动，例如在餐厅举办派对、卡丁车、保龄球等。即使你是一个铁杆内向的人并且对它们不感兴趣，你也应该知道会发生什么。 但社交活动并不是您可以期望一家公司举办的唯一活动。 询问黑客马拉松或某种内部知识共享会议。

概括

重复“不要害怕问”可以很好地总结我的建议列表；）。

将招聘会议视为招聘人员和您的信息来源。 在接受职位之前，请确保没有未知数。 如果您处于多个招聘流程中，这一点尤其重要，因为您不应该仅根据薪水来决定要约。 通过提出重要问题，您应该可以大致了解一家公司的经营状况，以及它是否适合您。

祝你下次工作面试好运，我相信你会给人留下好印象！



原文:

The recruitment processes can be weary and after answering countless questions you only think about leaving the interview, but you should never lose an opportunity to ask your interlocutors some questions. Why? There are a couple of reasons. First of all, there are a lot of things you should know before you accept the job offer. Another reason is more practical, when you are asking questions you look less intimidated and more experienced and so why not score some more points?

It’s important, however, to keep in mind that you shouldn't ask technical questions to HR people and vice versa, which can lead to uncomfortable situations. If you want to address any technical doubts you can ask if you may rise them on the next step of the recruitment process or maybe the recruiter can get you in touch with someone who can answer them.

I don’t want to crush you with an overwhelming list of all possible questions, because it may bring only more confusion. I present to you which topics you should stick to during the conversation to finish it with the most possible amount of information that should be handy.

1. Work organization

If you think about work organization, probably the first thing that comes to your mind is how your typical work day will look like. And that's the correct approach because that defines how your life will be going. Ask about hours, is it typical nine to five? Are there any office hours? Or maybe the company is totally flexible and you only should stick to the meetings? After you get the answer you will be sure if such day's composition suits you. So we already covered the meeting topic and to be frank - meetings can be harsh. Ask how many meetings you are scheduled to attend in a week. Some people prefer fewer meetings and if you are not sure what your preferences are - trust me, you prefer fewer ;)

Usually, based on the job offer, you already know if it is a remote, stationary or hybrid position, but even so, you should ask about that. Sometimes companies interpret remote jobs as “2 times a week in an office” or hybrid as “1 time a month from any location”. So better be sure than sorry. If you are expected frequently in an office, ask for free parking, because that can be a really big struggle in cities.

At this point you should know where and how you will work, so your next concern is equipment. Will the company provide you with a brand-new Macbook Pro? Can you expect to expand your home office to 8 monitors? Are peripherals even an option? Or will you need to use your old PC with a keyboard that is a house for a family of spiders?

2. Responsibilities and technicalities

Technical people should have technical responsibilities, that is obvious. Or at least it should be…but it’s not always the case. That’s why you should always ask about your responsibilities - what you need to do from starting working on a task till you mark it as “Done”. Will you be responsible for one project? Maybe besides the project, you are expected to handle something extra? Be inquisitive about it, and stick to this topic until your curiosity is satisfied.

After you finish the cross-examination about responsibilities, ask more specific questions. What does the technology stack look like? Which version of the framework is currently used? Unless you like working with legacy code, that is a good sign if a company uses one of the latest versions.

Another good idea is to ask about some advanced subjects. Is there Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery used? How big is the test coverage? Are you working in Test Driven Development? Such questions will get you some bonus points.

3. Team organization

After you get knowledge about your responsibilities, now is the time to ask about people who will be sharing them with you. Will you be working in a team? How big is the team? Do teams cooperate on the same product? What roles do people have in your team? It is really important to know that. Workflow varies significantly between a team of 5 programmers and a team of 3 programmers, QA and Product Manager.

The next step will be to ask about the methodology the team is working on. Is it Agile? Maybe Scrum? Waterfall? Or some in-house implementation of principles? Trust me it is more important than you can think. If the recruiter is avoiding the answer then you can assume that something is wrong with the team organization (or it's not for just one particular project!).

4. Your future

Your future should always be your concern, even if you are treating the company as a temporary stop. Courses, conferences, training, and certifications are the things that can positively affect your future and have a really good impact on your career. For junior developers such opportunities can be even more valuable than financial benefits. Ask if there is any budget for such activities.

On the other hand, consider asking about your career in the company. How often can you expect an evaluation meeting where you can ask about a pay raise? Can you get a promotion to higher positions? Don’t be afraid to ask that, it will not be considered rude, but rather appreciated that you are looking forward to developing and planning to build a career path.

5. Benefits and events

The last position on our list is focused on benefits and events. Benefits consider all kinds of bonuses you can expect from a company. For example, it can be private insurance, private healthcare, a lunch card or some kind of sports card. It varies between companies, some of them are giving you a lot of benefits, while others prefer giving you more on your paycheck.

Other things considered bonuses are company events. Such an event can be a typical activity that is helping build bonds with a team, so parties in restaurants, go-karts, bowling etc. Even if you are a hardcore introvert and you have no interest in them you should know what to expect. But social events are not the only activity you can expect a company to host. Ask about a hackathon or some kind of internal knowledge-sharing meeting.

Summary

Repeating “don’t be afraid to ask” can be a good summary of my pieces of advice list ;).

Treat recruitment meetings as a source of information, both for the recruiter and you. Before accepting a position be sure that there are no unknowns. This is especially important if you are in more than one recruitment process because you shouldn’t decide on an offer only salary-wise. Asking important questions should get you a brief understanding of how a company is doing and if it is the right place for you.

Good luck with your next job interview, I’m sure that you will make a good impression!

Items.Title: Blazor WebAssembly：渐进式 Web 应用程序的游戏规则改变者 | Blazor WebAssembly: A Game-Changer for Progressive Web Apps
Items.Description:
在 Web 开发领域，渐进式 Web 应用程序 (PWA) 越来越受欢迎，因为它们能够向用户提供类似原生应用程序的体验，同时又可以通过 Web 轻松访问。 然而，传统上开发 PWA 涉及使用不同技术的组合，这可能既复杂又耗时。

进入 Blazor WebAssembly，这是一个承诺使用 .NET 和 C# 编程语言简化 PWA 开发的框架。 它允许开发人员使用 .NET 创建功能齐全的交互式 Web 应用程序，这在以前只能通过服务器端开发来实现。

借助 Blazor WebAssembly，开发人员可以使用单一语言 C# 构建高性能、支持离线的 PWA。 该框架提供了一组 UI 组件，可以定制这些组件以满足应用程序的特定需求，从而实现快速开发。 使用 Blazor WebAssembly 进行 PWA 开发的显着优势之一是它能够提供比传统 PWA 更快的加载时间和更好的性能。 这是因为 Blazor WebAssembly 将 C# 代码编译成 WebAssembly，后者在浏览器中本机运行。 这种方法消除了下载和解析 JavaScript 代码的需要，从而缩短了启动时间并提供了更流畅的用户体验。

此外，Blazor WebAssembly 提供了代码重用的优势，允许开发人员在客户端和服务器之间共享代码。 这会缩短开发时间并提高代码可维护性。

它使用 .NET 和 C# 编程语言，允许开发人员使用单一语言创建快速、高性能的 PWA。 凭借其易用性、更好的性能和代码重用能力，Blazor WebAssembly 无疑值得您在下一个 PWA 项目中考虑。

原文:

In the world of web development, progressive web apps (PWAs) have been gaining popularity due to their ability to deliver native app-like experiences to users while being easily accessible through the web. However, developing PWAs traditionally has involved using a combination of different technologies, which can be complex and time-consuming.

Enter Blazor WebAssembly, a framework that promises to simplify PWA development using .NET and C# programming languages. Blazor WebAssembly is a client-side web development framework that runs C# code in the browser. It allows developers to create fully functional, interactive web applications using .NET, which previously was only possible through server-side development.

With Blazor WebAssembly, developers can build high-performance, offline-capable PWAs using a single language, C#. The framework provides a set of UI components that can be customized to fit the application's specific needs, allowing for rapid development. Blazor also enables easy integration with other .NET technologies, such as ASP.NET and Azure.

One of the significant advantages of using Blazor WebAssembly for PWA development is its ability to provide faster load times and better performance than traditional PWAs. This is because Blazor WebAssembly compiles the C# code into WebAssembly, which runs natively in the browser. This approach eliminates the need to download and parse JavaScript code, resulting in faster startup times and a smoother user experience.

Moreover, Blazor WebAssembly offers the benefit of code reuse, allowing developers to share code between the client and server. This results in reduced development time and better code maintainability.

Blazor WebAssembly is a game-changer for PWA development. Its use of .NET and C# programming languages allows developers to create fast, high-performance PWAs with a single language. With its ease of use, better performance, and code reuse capabilities, Blazor WebAssembly is undoubtedly worth considering for your next PWA project.

Items.Title: 使用 RTK 查询轻松获取数据：综合指南 | Fetching Data Made Easy with RTK Query: A Comprehensive Guide
Items.Description:
考纳哈桑

发表于 4 月 20 日

从 API 获取数据可能是一项乏味且耗时的任务，但使用正确的工具和技术，可以使它变得更容易、更高效。 一个这样的工具是 Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query)，它是一个强大的库，可以简化 Redux 应用程序的数据获取和缓存。

在本文中，我们将探讨如何使用 RTK 查询从 API 获取数据，以及它如何让您的生活更轻松。 我们还将提供代码示例以帮助您入门。

入门

在深入研究代码之前，了解什么是 RTK 查询及其工作原理很重要。 它会自动缓存来自您的 API 请求的响应，这有助于减少请求的数量并提高应用程序的性能。

它可以处理嵌套数据结构，并将响应数据规范化为更易于使用的规范化数据结构。

现在我们对 RTK 查询有了基本的了解，让我们看看如何使用它从 API 获取数据。

获取数据

使用 Create React App 创建一个新的 React 应用程序：

 npx create-react-app my-app cd my-app

安装所需的依赖项：

 npm install @reduxjs/toolkit react-redux @reduxjs/toolkit/query axios

  • 创建一个新的 RTK 查询 API 切片：

    在src目录中创建一个名为api.js的新文件，内容如下：

     import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'; import axios from 'axios'; export const apiSlice = createApi({ reducerPath: 'api', baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/', }), endpoints: (builder) => ({ getPosts: builder.query({ query: () => 'posts', }), }), tagTypes: ['Post'], endpoints: (builder) => ({ getPost: builder.query({ query: (id) => `posts/${id}`, transformResponse: (response) => ({ ...response, tag: 'Post', }), providesTags: (result, error, id) => [{ type: 'Post', id }], }), }), }); export const { useGetPostsQuery, useGetPostQuery } = apiSlice;

    • 创建一个新的 Redux 存储：

      在src目录中创建一个名为store.js的新文件，内容如下：

       import { configureStore } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'; import { apiSlice } from './api'; export const store = configureStore({ reducer: { [apiSlice.reducerPath]: apiSlice.reducer, }, middleware: (getDefaultMiddleware) => getDefaultMiddleware().concat(apiSlice.middleware), });

      • 在 React 组件中使用 RTK 查询挂钩：

        使用以下内容在 src 目录中创建一个名为App.js的新文件：

         import { useGetPostsQuery, useGetPostQuery } from './api'; function App() { const { data: posts, isLoading: isLoadingPosts, error: errorPosts } = useGetPostsQuery(); const { data: post, isLoading: isLoadingPost, error: errorPost } = useGetPostQuery(1); return ( <div> <h1>Posts</h1> {isLoadingPosts && <p>Loading posts...</p>} {errorPosts && <p>Error loading posts: {errorPosts}</p>} {posts && posts.map((post) => ( <div key={post.id}> <h2>{post.title}</h2> <p>{post.body}</p> </div> ))} <h1>Post 1</h1> {isLoadingPost && <p>Loading post...</p>} {errorPost && <p>Error loading post: {errorPost}</p>} {post && ( <div> <h2>{post.title}</h2> <p>{post.body}</p> </div> )} </div> ); } export default App;
        • 运行 React 应用程序：

          结论

          总之，RTK Query 是一个强大的库，可以简化 Redux 应用程序的数据获取和缓存。 它提供了一组钩子和实用程序，可以轻松地从 API 获取数据并在 Redux 存储中管理它们的状态。

原文:

Kauna Hassan

Posted on Apr 20

   

Fetching data from an API can be a tedious and time-consuming task, but with the right tools and techniques, it can be made easier and more efficient. One such tool is Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query), which is a powerful library that simplifies data fetching and caching for Redux applications.

In this article, we will explore how to use RTK Query to fetch data from an API and how it can make your life easier. We will also provide code samples to help you get started.

Getting Started

Before we dive into the code, it's important to understand what RTK Query is and how it works. RTK Query is a library that provides a set of hooks and utilities to help you fetch data from APIs and manage their state in your Redux store.

One of the key features of RTK Query is its caching mechanism. It automatically caches the responses from your API requests, which helps to reduce the number of requests made and improve the performance of your application.

Another important feature of RTK Query is its ability to handle complex API responses. It can handle nested data structures and can normalise the response data into a normalised data structure that is easier to work with.

Now that we have a basic understanding of RTK Query, let's see how we can use it to fetch data from an API.

Fetching Data

Create a new React app using Create React App:

npx create-react-app my-app
cd my-app

Install the required dependencies:

npm install @reduxjs/toolkit react-redux @reduxjs/toolkit/query axios

  • @reduxjs/toolkit provides the necessary tools for creating the Redux store and slices.

  • react-redux provides the necessary components for integrating Redux with React.

  • @reduxjs/toolkit/query provides the necessary tools for using RTK Query.

  • axios is a popular library for making HTTP requests.

Create a new RTK Query API slice:

Create a new file called api.js in the src directory with the following content:

import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query';
import axios from 'axios'; export const apiSlice = createApi({ reducerPath: 'api', baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/', }), endpoints: (builder) => ({ getPosts: builder.query({ query: () => 'posts', }), }), tagTypes: ['Post'], endpoints: (builder) => ({ getPost: builder.query({ query: (id) => `posts/${id}`, transformResponse: (response) => ({ ...response, tag: 'Post', }), providesTags: (result, error, id) => [{ type: 'Post', id }], }), }),
}); export const { useGetPostsQuery, useGetPostQuery } = apiSlice;

  • The createApi function creates a new RTK Query API slice.
    reducerPath specifies the name of the Redux store slice for the API.

  • baseQuery specifies the function for making HTTP requests to the API.

  • endpoints specifies the API endpoints that can be used by RTK Query.

  • tagTypes specifies the different types of tags that can be used to cache API responses.

  • transformResponse specifies a function to transform the API response before it is stored in the cache.

  • providesTags specifies the tags that should be used to cache the API response.

Create a new Redux store:

Create a new file called store.js in the src directory with the following content:

import { configureStore } from '@reduxjs/toolkit';
import { apiSlice } from './api'; export const store = configureStore({ reducer: { [apiSlice.reducerPath]: apiSlice.reducer, }, middleware: (getDefaultMiddleware) => getDefaultMiddleware().concat(apiSlice.middleware),
});

  • configureStore creates a new Redux store.

  • The reducer property specifies the reducers used by the store.

  • The middleware property specifies the middleware used by the store.

Use the RTK Query hooks in your React components:

Create a new file called App.js in the src directory with the following content:

import { useGetPostsQuery, useGetPostQuery } from './api'; function App() { const { data: posts, isLoading: isLoadingPosts, error: errorPosts } = useGetPostsQuery(); const { data: post, isLoading: isLoadingPost, error: errorPost } = useGetPostQuery(1); return ( <div> <h1>Posts</h1>
 {isLoadingPosts && <p>Loading posts...</p>}
 {errorPosts && <p>Error loading posts: {errorPosts}</p>}
 {posts && posts.map((post) => ( <div key={post.id}> <h2>{post.title}</h2>
 <p>{post.body}</p>
 </div>
 ))} <h1>Post 1</h1>
 {isLoadingPost && <p>Loading post...</p>}
 {errorPost && <p>Error loading post: {errorPost}</p>}
 {post && ( <div> <h2>{post.title}</h2>
 <p>{post.body}</p>
 </div>
 )} </div>
 );
} export default App;
  • The useGetPostsQuery hook is used to fetch all the posts from the API.
  • The useGetPostQuery hook is used to fetch a specific post from the API.
  • The data property contains the data returned by the API.
  • The isLoading property indicates whether the API is currently loading.
  • The error property contains the error returned by the API.

Run the React app:

Conclusion

In conclusion, RTK Query is a powerful library that simplifies data fetching and caching for Redux applications. It provides a set of hooks and utilities that make it easy to fetch data from APIs and manage their state in your Redux store.

如果您正在使用 React.js 构建旅游网站或应用程序，您可能需要为您的用户访问实时航班信息。 航班数据 API 是一个很好的解决方案，因为它提供了各种航空公司和目的地的最新航班时刻表、可用性和价格。

在本文中，我们将探索如何在 React.js 应用程序中使用 Flight Data API，我们将提供一个示例来说明如何使用 Axios 库来使用 API。

飞行数据 API 入门

在您开始在 React.js 应用程序中使用航班数据 API 之前，您需要注册一个 TravelPayouts 帐户并获取一个 API 密钥。 获得 API 密钥后，您就可以开始向 API 发出请求。

在 React 组件中使用带有 Axios 的航班数据 API

要在 React 组件中将 Flight Data API 与 Axios 结合使用，您需要安装 Axios 并将其导入到您的组件中。

以下是如何在 React 组件中使用 Axios 向 Flight Data API 发出请求的示例：

 import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react'; import axios from 'axios'; function FlightData() { const [flightData, setFlightData] = useState(null); const [error, setError] = useState(null); useEffect(() => { const options = { method: 'GET', url: 'https://travelpayouts-travelpayouts-flight-data-v1.p.rapidapi.com/v1/prices/direct/', params: {destination: 'LED', origin: 'MOW'}, headers: { 'X-Access-Token': '<YOUR_API_KEY>', 'X-RapidAPI-Key': '<YOUR_RAPIDAPI_KEY>', 'X-RapidAPI-Host': 'travelpayouts-travelpayouts-flight-data-v1.p.rapidapi.com' } }; axios.request(options) .then(function (response) { setFlightData(response.data); }) .catch(function (error) { setError(error); }); }, []); if (error) return <div>Error: {error.message}</div>; if (!flightData) return <div>Loading flight data...</div>; return ( <div> <h2>Flight Data</h2> <pre>{JSON.stringify(flightData, null, 2)}</pre> </div> ); } export default FlightData;

在此示例中，我们使用useState和useEffect挂钩来管理组件的状态。 我们将flightData初始化为null并将error初始化为null ，并且我们在 useEffect 挂钩中使用 Axios 向 Flight Data API 发出请求。

如果 API 请求成功，则将响应数据存储在flightData中，然后使用pre标签将其呈现到屏幕上。 如果 API 请求有错误，错误将被存储在error中并呈现到屏幕上。

结论

将航班数据 API 与 React.js 结合使用可以帮助您向用户提供实时航班信息。 按照上面的示例，您可以轻松地使用 Axios 向 API 发出请求，并将结果显示在您的 React 组件中。 请记住始终正确处理错误和速率限制，并确保您的 API 密钥安全。 借助航班数据 API，您可以增强旅游网站或应用程序的用户体验，并帮助您的用户找到满足他们需求的最佳航班。

If you are building a travel website or application using React.js, you may need to access real-time flight information for your users. The Flight Data API is a great solution for this, as it provides up-to-date flight schedules, availability, and prices for a wide range of airlines and destinations.

In this article, we will explore how to use the Flight Data API in a React.js application, and we will provide an example of how to use the API using the Axios library.

Getting Started with the Flight Data API

Before you can start using the Flight Data API in your React.js application, you will need to sign up for a TravelPayouts account and obtain an API key. Once you have your API key, you can start making requests to the API.

Using the Flight Data API with Axios in a React Component

Axios is a popular JavaScript library for making HTTP requests, and it is often used in React.js applications. To use the Flight Data API with Axios in a React component, you will need to install Axios and import it into your component.

Here is an example of how to make a request to the Flight Data API using Axios in a React component:

import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import axios from 'axios'; function FlightData() { const [flightData, setFlightData] = useState(null); const [error, setError] = useState(null); useEffect(() => { const options = { method: 'GET', url: 'https://travelpayouts-travelpayouts-flight-data-v1.p.rapidapi.com/v1/prices/direct/', params: {destination: 'LED', origin: 'MOW'}, headers: { 'X-Access-Token': '<YOUR_API_KEY>', 'X-RapidAPI-Key': '<YOUR_RAPIDAPI_KEY>', 'X-RapidAPI-Host': 'travelpayouts-travelpayouts-flight-data-v1.p.rapidapi.com' } }; axios.request(options) .then(function (response) { setFlightData(response.data); }) .catch(function (error) { setError(error); }); }, []); if (error) return <div>Error: {error.message}</div>;
 if (!flightData) return <div>Loading flight data...</div>;
 return ( <div> <h2>Flight Data</h2>
 <pre>{JSON.stringify(flightData, null, 2)}</pre>
 </div>
 );
} export default FlightData;

In this example, we are using the useState and useEffect hooks to manage the state of our component. We are initializing flightData to null and error to null, and we are making a request to the Flight Data API using Axios inside the useEffect hook.

If the API request is successful, the response data is stored in flightData, which is then rendered to the screen using the pre tag. If there is an error with the API request, the error is stored in error and rendered to the screen.

Conclusion

Using the Flight Data API with React.js can help you provide real-time flight information to your users. By following the example above, you can easily make requests to the API using Axios and display the results in your React components. Remember to always handle errors and rate limits properly, and to keep your API keys secure. With the Flight Data API, you can enhance the user experience of your travel website or application and help your users find the best flights for their needs.

它教会开发人员如何使用 API 及其功能。 良好的文档对于确保开发人员能够快速将 API 集成到他们的项目中、节省时间和精力至关重要。

编写有效 API 文档的最佳实践

  • 保持简洁： API 文档应该简明扼要。 它应该解释 API 的功能、它接受的参数以及它返回的响应。 避免添加可能会使使用 API 的开发人员感到困惑的不必要信息。

  • 使用清晰的语言：使用开发人员容易理解的简单语言。 避免使用会使读者感到困惑的技术术语或复杂术语。

  • 组织文档：以清晰和合乎逻辑的方式组织文档。 提供目录或索引，以帮助开发人员快速找到所需的信息。

  • 使用示例：使用示例来说明 API 的工作原理。 这可以帮助开发人员更好地了解 API 的功能以及如何在他们的项目中使用它。

  • 包含错误消息：确保包含错误消息以及如何处理它们。 这可以帮助开发人员解决使用 API 时可能出现的任何问题。

  • 使文档保持最新：随着 API 的更改，使 API 文档保持最新。 这可确保开发人员始终能够访问最准确和最相关的信息。

编写有效 API 文档的工具

有许多方法可以创建出色的 API 文档。 这里有一些例子：

  • 它提供了一个易于使用的界面，使生成和管理文档变得简单。

  • 该文档使用简单的降价语言创建，使其易于理解和使用。

  • 它使您能够编写包含示例请求和响应的详细文档。

结论

为了让开发人员理解 API 的功能以及如何在他们的项目中使用它，API 文档至关重要。 通过坚持最佳实践和使用适当的工具，您可以生成高效的文档，从而节省开发人员的时间和精力。 请记住，文档应该简短、清晰、逻辑组织，包括示例和错误消息，并保持最新。 通过采取适当的方法，您可以确保您的 API 易于开发人员使用和集成。

原文:

Kauna Hassan

Posted on Apr 20

   

API documentation is an essential component of every software development project, particularly when developing web services. It teaches developers how to utilize an API and what its capabilities are. Good documentation is critical for ensuring that developers can quickly integrate an API into their project, saving time and effort.

Best Practices for Writing Effective API Documentation

  • Keep it concise: API documentation should be concise and to the point. It should explain the API's functionality, the parameters it accepts, and the responses it returns. Avoid adding unnecessary information that can confuse the developers using the API.

  • Use clear language: Use simple language that developers can understand easily. Avoid using technical jargon or complicated terms that can confuse the reader.

  • Organize the documentation: Organize the documentation in a clear and logical manner. Provide a table of contents or an index to help developers find the information they need quickly.

  • Use examples: Use examples to illustrate how the API works. This can help developers understand the API's functionality better and how to use it in their projects.

  • Include error messages: Make sure to include error messages and how to handle them. This can help developers troubleshoot any issues that may arise while using the API.

  • Keep the documentation up-to-date: Keep the API documentation up-to-date as changes are made to the API. This ensures that developers always have access to the most accurate and relevant information.

Tools for Writing Effective API Documentation

There are numerous methods for creating excellent API documentation. Here are a few examples:

  • Swagger: Swagger is an open-source platform for creating, designing, and documenting RESTful APIs. It offers an easy-to-use interface that makes it simple to produce and manage documentation.

  • API Blueprint: Another open-source tool for creating succinct and easy-to-read API documentation is API Blueprint. The documentation is created using a simple markdown language, making it simple to understand and use.

  • Postman: Postman is a well-known API development tool with documentation features. It enables you to write detailed documentation with sample requests and responses.

Conclusion

For developers to comprehend an API's functionality and how to use it in their projects, API documentation is crucial. You may produce efficient documentation that will save developers time and effort by adhering to best practices and using the appropriate tools. Keep in mind that the documentation should be brief, clear, logically organized, include examples and error messages, and be kept up to date. You can make sure that your API is simple for developers to use and integrate by taking the appropriate approach.
